coredata.dc:title: Enzymolysis-treated wood-derived hierarchical porous carbon for fluorescence-functionalized phase change materials

coredata.dc:description: Dominating obstacles that currently obstruct the development and industrial application of phase change materials (PCMs) are their low thermal conductivity, high cost, easy leakage and poor mechanical performance. Our goal is to enhance the thermal conductivity and power capacity of PCMs as much as possible using abundant biomass-derived carbon as supports. Herein, cellulase is employed to efficiently hydrolyze segmental cellulose in wood to formulate luxuriant micropores, which contribute to sufficiently expose the interior of the wood to effectively construct luxuriant adsorption sites onto the carbon skeleton during the subsequent high-temperature pyrolysis process. The resulting wood-derived hierarchical porous carbon infiltrates polyethylene glycol (PEG) and sufficiently liberates crystallization via microscopic morphology regulation. The strategy assembles good mechanical performance, high energy storage capacity (151.74 J/g), anisotropic thermal conductivity and improved thermal stability. Furthermore, the composite PCMs loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) integrate the dual advantages of fluorescent function and thermal energy storage, which provides the possibility for the application of enzymolysis-treated wood-supported composite PCMs in specific equipment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dominating obstacles that currently obstruct the development and industrial application of phase change materials (PCMs) are their low thermal conductivity, high cost, easy leakage and poor mechanical performance. Our goal is to enhance the thermal conductivity and power capacity of PCMs as much as possible using abundant biomass-derived carbon as supports. Herein, cellulase is employed to efficiently hydrolyze segmental cellulose in wood to formulate luxuriant micropores, which contribute to sufficiently expose the interior of the wood to effectively construct luxuriant adsorption sites onto the carbon skeleton during the subsequent high-temperature pyrolysis process. The resulting wood-derived hierarchical porous carbon infiltrates polyethylene glycol (PEG) and sufficiently liberates crystallization via microscopic morphology regulation. The strategy assembles good mechanical performance, high energy storage capacity (151.74 J/g), anisotropic thermal conductivity and improved thermal stability. Furthermore, the composite PCMs loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) integrate the dual advantages of fluorescent function and thermal energy storage, which provides the possibility for the application of enzymolysis-treated wood-supported composite PCMs in specific equipment.

ce:section.ce:para: Phase change materials (PCMs), as an important way of energy storage, have emerged as a promising energy storage technology for coping with future energy demands in virtue of its ultrahigh energy storage density and suitable phase transition temperature during the phase transition process [1–4]. However, pure PCMs inevitably encounter the challenges of leakage and low thermal conductivity in their direct applications, which tremendously restrain their practical energy storage efficiency and hinder their further widespread application [5,6]. Therefore, porous supporting materials are introduced into the phase change energy storage system, and it is anticipated that the capillary force, surface tension and other interactions in carriers can restrict the phase change core material in channels, thus settling the traditional leakage problem [7,8]. Nevertheless, restricted by the inherent nanoconfinement effect of supporting materials, the melting and freezing enthalpies of the composite PCMs are usually lower than that of bulk PCMs. Another momentous obstacle to pure PCMs is their inherent low thermal conductivity, which will inevitably result in a hysteretic thermal response. Currently, the most frequently-used method to improve the thermal conductivity of PCMs is to assemble PCMs with thermally conductive materials to fabricate composite PCMs [9,10], including metallics [11,12], metal foams [13,14], carbon nanotube [15], and graphene [16–18]. Nevertheless, preliminary studies have revealed that thermal conductivity additives and energy storage density are in a competitory relationship [19,20], that is, the greater the additive content, the lower the energy storage density. Moreover, restricting by the interfacial effect, the thermally conductive additives during thermal cycling will ineluctably fall off from the composite PCMs [21]. Recently, 3D network carbon as a representative candidate supporting materials with high thermal conductivity has seized extensive attention, which can furnish prominent interconnection paths for the high-efficiency propagation of phonons [22,23]. Nevertheless, its immanent shortcomings of complicated manufacture process, exorbitant cost and low yield inescapably restrict its large-scale application in the field of energy storage.

In order to efficaciously resolve the aforementioned problems, researchers have turned their attention to biomass materials with abundant source, high cost-effectiveness, renewability and environmental friendliness, which have luxuriant pore structure after carbonization, and their thermal conductivity can be tremendously promoted [8]. Currently, the application of biomass-based porous carbon materials to the manufacture of shape-stabilized composite phase change materials (ss-CPCMs) has become one of the development tendencies [24,25]. Wood as a renewable resource has inartificial pore structure with clear and methodic structure, and the aperture size ranges from millimeters to nanometers, which can be employed as prominent supporting materials for PCMs [26,27]. Yang et al. employed carbonized wood (CW) as supports to encapsulate 1-tetradecanol (TD) with a load rate of 73.4%, and the thermal conductivity of the achieved composites in axial direction is 0.669 Wm−1K−1 at 50 °C, which is 114% times that of pure TD [28]. Rudimentary researches have demonstrated that the pre-treatment step of delignification of wood can promote the adsorption capacity of wood-based porous carbon [29–31]. Liu et al. removed lignin and hemicellulose from wood to acquire low-density wood with fascinating structure, and employed the chemically-treated wood as carriers to encapsulate paraffin wax (PW) [32]. The loading rate of composites is as high as 84%, and the result is superior to the loading rate of untreated wood. Moreover, Ma et al. [33] and Yang et al. [34] also demonstrated that the delignified wood as supporting materials can achieve ss-CPCMs with prominent performance. Nevertheless, the energy storage efficiency of composite PCMs achieved by employing delignified wood-based porous carbon as carriers has not yet reached the requirements of actual industrial applications. Hence, how to further promote the porosity of wood-based porous carbon and achieve the qualitative leap in energy storage efficiency of composite PCMs still remains an enormous challenge.

Enzymes as high-performance catalysts have prominent specificity to the reaction products, and it can still maintain its activity even under moderate and innoxious conditions. Enzymatic hydrolysis of cellulose-containing biomass materials can efficiently hydrolyze part of the cellulose matrix, thereby forming luxuriant micropores on the surface of the pore wall to maximize its specific surface area [35]. Fabricating ultra-low density porous carbon with cellulose-rich wood as a precursor is a very wise choice, since it is an inartificial support of cellulose and it consists several low-tortuosity channels [36]. When the cellulase is employed to partially hydrolyze the wood-based cellulose, a hierarchically interconnected porous structure including luxurious micropores/mesopores/macropores will be formulated on the low-curvature channels, which is beneficial to being employed as supporting materials to manufacture composite PCMs with prominent storage energy density.

Herein, a template-free, top-down method is exploited for manufacturing 3D wood-derived hierarchical porous carbon applying in prominent thermal energy harvesting. Concretely, a straightforward and environmentally friendly approach is designed to manufacture hierarchical porous carbon materials with ultra-high porosity from wood without requiring any artificial activator, employing high-efficiency biological process on account of enzyme chemistry. Cellulase selectively enzymatically disintegrates cellulose in wood and retains lignin in it, thereby maintaining the unabridged anisotropic structure of wood. Afterwards, the pre-processed wood by enzymes is carbonized by high-temperature pyrolysis to achieve self-supporting 3D hierarchical porous network carbon with distinguished mechanical strength. Regulatory microcosmic aperture structure is achieved by the cooperative effect of enzymatic hydrolysis and high-temperature pyrolysis. The manufactured composite PCMs assemble high energy storage capacity (151.74 J/g) and increased thermal conductivity (increased by 31.23%), which can be attributed to the consecutive thermal transmission path and increased mean free path of phonon transmission in the 3D wood-derived hierarchical porous carbon [37,38]. Moreover, the resulting enzymolysis-treated wood-based composite PCMs loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) effectively provide the probability to design and fabricate devices with particular multi-functional requirements. Overall, the aforementioned enzymolysis-induced strategy has promising prospects in exploiting porous carbon nanomaterials with regulatable microscopic morphology and devising energy storage devices with prominent energy storage density, captivating fluorescence functions, high-efficiency light-to-thermal and electric-to-thermal conversion capabilities.

Bass wood was purchased from Haolin Industrial Co., Ltd. Cellulase was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), glucose, PEG2000 and other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Macleans Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

The delignification of the original wood was processed in accordance with the previous literature [39]. The achieved delignificated wood was immersed in an aqueous solution containing 500 U g−1 of cellulase (enzymatic activity unit) and incubated at 40 °C for 24 h [40]. The enzymolysis-treatment wood was washed to neutrality by deionized water (pH ≈ 7.0), and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

In order to demonstrate the superiority of the delignification and enzymolysis-treated procedures, the raw wood, the delignified wood, and the delignified and enzyme-treated wood were calcined at different temperatures (700 °C, 800 °C and 900 °C) for 1 h. The sample are denoted as CW-x, CW-D-x or CW-E-x, where CW represents the carbonated wood, D represents the delignification treatment, E represents the enzymolysis treatment after delignification treatment, and x represents different calcination temperature.

The formation of CQDs was achieved according to previous literature [41]. Typically, 1.44 g glucose was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water, and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for 10 min. The glucose aqueous solution or wood-derived porous carbon/glucose aqueous mixture were put into the reaction kettle, and reacted at 180 °C for 24 h. The consequent composites were freeze-dried at 70 °C for 24 h, then collected and denoted as CQDs/CW-E-x.

A representative vacuum impregnation method was adopted to prepare composite PCMs. First of all, pure PEG and wood-derived porous carbon were put in a glass beaker and transferred to a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the product was placed in an oven at 80 °C, and the excess liquid organic PCMs on the outside of the composites were adsorbed and removed with filter paper. The filter paper needs to be replaced successively throughout the process until no leakage occurs.

SEM (SU8010, Japan Hitachi) and TEM (JEM-2100F) were used to observe the morphologies and the elemental mapping analysis of the achieved composites. The structure and phase of the composites can be acquired by XRD (M21X, Cu Ka radiation, λ = 1.541 Å). The functional groups of samples can be defined by FT-IR spectra (Nicolet 6700). The composition and binding energy of the PCMs can be evaluated by XPS (ESCALAB 250Xi instrument). TGA (NETZSCH STA449F3, Germany) was used to evaluate the thermal stability of the composites, and was performed from 40 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. The DSC (Q2000) was employed to evaluate the enthalpies of the composites. Thermal imaging camera FLIR ONE Pro was employed to register the temperature response of composites during the heating process. Multimeter was used to measure the horizontal and vertical resistance of porous carbon. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was used to measure the fluorescence properties of the materials. The photo-thermal conversion properties of the composites were tested by using a Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300D) to simulate a light source. The electro-thermal conversion performance of the composites was tested by applying a voltage to the sample through a DC power supply (MS-1003D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and performance of supports
ce:section.ce:para: Enzymolysis-treatment and high-temperature pyrolysis carbonization integrally transform the wood into 3D network carbon materials, or further load CQDs harvesting the fluorescence function, and afterwards encapsulate small organic molecules to manufacture composite PCMs (Scheme 1 ). The untreated wood needs to be delignified, and then the cellulase enzyme is introduced for inducing enzymatic hydrolysis. Cellulase contains three components: endo-1,4-d-glucanase, exo-1,4-β-d-glucanase, 3-d-glucosidase [42]. Generally, diverse enzyme components synergistically hydrolyze insoluble cellulose substrates by providing sites for each other in the process of cellulase hydrolysis, reducing barriers to action, and reducing product inhibition [43,44]. Delightedly, cellulase can partially hydrolyze the cellulose in the wood, which not only guarantees that the inherent mechanical properties of the material are not destroyed, but also maximizes the pores on the wood surface and inside the channel, benefiting the construction of luxuriant adsorption sites. The synergistic effect of enzymolysis-treatment and high-temperature pyrolysis realizes the construction of microscopic pore structure of porous carbon. Here, the efficient regulation of the microscopic morphology of carbon materials is realized by adjusting the temperature of high-temperature pyrolysis [40]. Moreover, cellulase contains slight nitrogen causing the pore structure to be further activated during the carbonization process.

Enzymolysis-treatment and high-temperature pyrolysis have a significant influence on the structural transformation of the wood. As shown in Fig. 1 a, Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, CW-D-x and CW-E-x both have the highly oriented macroporous structure with a main diameter of 20–40 μm and smooth macroporous surface. Cellulase has the ability to selectively hydrolyze cellulose molecules. Enzymolysis-treatment causes the internal and external wall of the wood to become rougher with many shallow nanopores (Fig. 1d). The enlarged TEM images in Fig. 1b and e further reveal that there are a large number of interconnected nanopores on the surface of the enzymolysis-treated wood wall, most of which are in the range of a few nanometers to tens of nanometers. During the fabrication process, it was found that the delignification and enzymolysis-treatment process did not destroy the integral shape of the wood (2
×
2 cm2), while the subsequent pyrolysis process would cause slight volume shrinkage. Relievedly, the entire mechanical strength was well maintained during the manufacture process (Fig. S3). Entertainingly, the carbonized wood block has prominent mechanical strength and can support 461 times the weight (Fig. S4).

Furthermore, multimeter was employed to measure the electrical resistivity of porous carbon in the vertical or horizontal direction (Fig. 1f), and the electrical conductivity per unit length of the porous carbon in the vertical and horizontal directions were calculated to be 0.031 and 0.004 S m−1, respectively, which illustrate that the electron conduction efficiency in the vertical direction is much better than that in the horizontal direction under external field excitation. In general, the electron thermal transmission mechanism caused by the existence of plentiful free electrons would synergistically promote the phonon thermal transmission from the lattice vibration of solid materials. Therefore, it can be inferred that the thermal conductivity in the vertical direction is much better than that in the horizontal direction.

Besides, to further verify the inference of the anisotropic thermal conductivity of hierarchical porous carbon after enzymatic hydrolysis, the samples were placed on a hot plate at 80 °C, and the instantaneous temperature response of the samples during the temperature increase process was recorded by a paperless recorder. Unquestionably, the thermal transfer rate in the vertical direction is conspicuously higher than that in the horizontal direction, and the phenomenon can be ascribed to the heat convection in the oriented pores (Fig. S5). Summarily, the self-supporting 3D porous carbon based on the porous structure of inartificial wood makes sufficient utilization of the wood's thermal conductivity, 3D interconnected network and natural ion transport channels, which dexterously circumvents the employ of traditional chemical or physical activation methods that have the disadvantages of requiring toxic reagents and cockamamie preparation processes.

Enzymolysis-treatment and high-temperature pyrolysis will synergistically influence the transformation of wood's porous structure, and the regulation of micro-morphology is performed by controlling the pyrolysis temperature. As shown in Fig. 1, Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, wood-derived porous carbon has a highly oriented macroporous structure, and their dominating diameter is about 40 μm. Moreover, some holes with a diameter of about 13 μm are evenly distributed around the macropores. And these pore structures can still be well maintained after calcination at 700–900 °C. As previously mentioned, cellulase has the capacity to selectively hydrolyze cellulose molecules. Compared with the achieved porous carbon without enzymolysis-treatment, the enzymolysis-treatment process apparently causes the inner wall of the wood to become scabrous, because abundant shallow nanopores appear (Fig. 1d). The TEM images further reveal that a large number of interconnected nanopores appeared on the surface of the enzymolysis-treated wood after high-temperature pyrolysis, most of which are in the range of a few nanometers to tens of nanometers (Fig. 1b and e). Element mapping employing energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) illustrates the homogeneous distribution of C and O elements in the porous carbon (Fig. 1c and Table S1).

CQDs are carbon-based zero-dimensional materials with excellent optical properties, good water solubility, environmental friendliness, low cost, and good biocompatibility. Presently, CQDs have been extensively applied in medical imaging, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, and so on. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs into the field of phase change energy storage is of great significance for the development of novel multifunctional energy storage materials. In order to better explore the CQDs/wood-derived porous carbon, we performed a particular characterization of the achieved CQDs. Generally, the ground state of the substance absorbs energy and transitions to an excited state when CQDs are irradiated by sunlight, and the excited state releases energy through radiation to return to the ground state, and the released energy appears in the form of fluorescence, and the involved process realizes the photoluminescence of CQDs. In brief, the luminescence phenomenon caused by the transition between energy levels endows CQDs superior performance. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to further detect the surface groups of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the stretching vibration of C–OH appears at 3426 cm−1, the stretching vibrations of –CH3/-CH2 appear at 2919 and 2850 cm−1, respectively, and the stretching vibration of CO appears at 1617 cm−1 [45]. In addition, the stretching vibrations of C–O–C appear at 1384 and 1203 cm−1, and the stretching vibrations of C–H appear at 1060-1166 cm−1. The above analysis reveals that the surface of CQDs is rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which is conducive to their use as guest molecules to decorate wood-based porous carbon materials. XRD was employed to characterize freeze-dried CQDs powder, as shown in Fig. 2b. There is a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.2°, and the interplanar spacing (0.412 nm) is greater than the (020) interplanar spacing (0.273 nm) of graphite, which may be due to the increased (020) interplanar spacing by a large amount of H element in the sample. XPS was applied to measure the element composition and binding configurations of manufactured CQDs (Fig. 2c and d). In the high-resolution C1s spectra of CQDs, the binding energy peaks at 284.8, 286.2 and 286.7 eV are assigned to SP2 CC, C atoms in C–OH, and C–O, respectively. Benefiting from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs, CQDs can be efficiently loaded onto wood-derived porous carbon surfaces via hydrogen bonding, as evidenced by XPS spectroscopy (Fig. S6). The UV-absorption spectrum of CQDs is shown in Fig. 2e. In order to further study the optical properties of CQDs, the emission spectra of CQDs excited by different wavelengths were explored. As shown in Fig. 2f, CQDs have obvious absorption at about 429 nm in UV–vis absorption spectrum, and it can emit bright blue light when excited by ultraviolet light. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs first increases and then decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength, reaching a maximum under excitation at 340 nm wavelength. The phenomenon may be that the probability of radiative transition increases as the excitation wavelength increases. However, the probability of non-radiative transition increases when the excitation wavelength is further increased and brings some energy loss, leading to the quenching of luminescence. Besides, the position of the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs is almost not affected by the change of excitation wavelength, indicating that the achieved CQDs have uniform fluorescence emission points and homogeneous particle size distribution [46]. Currently, the luminescence mechanism of CQDs is still unclear, and it is comprehensively regarded that the luminescence originates from a certain kind of defect, that is, the defects existing on the surface of CQDs are employed as excitation energy traps to emit light under the excitation of a certain wavelength of light [47,48]. The formation and surface passivation of fluorescent CQDs manufactured by hydrolyzing glucose occur simultaneously in the process of hydrothermal carbonization. Compared with the complicated purification and separation and time-consuming surface passivation processes of other synthetic methods, the method has characteristic advantages such as convenient and controllable, low preparation cost, green and clean, and can improve the activity of reactants. The surface of CQDs has abundant functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which can act on the surface of CQDs to form diverse defects, so that the CQDs exhibit different optical properties under different fabrication conditions. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs confirmed the photoluminescence properties of CQDs. Therefore, we expect that the composite PCMs loaded with CQDs can be applied in the field of optoelectronic devices in the future. Noticeably, PCMs can effectively shorten the temperature fluctuation range of optoelectronic devices, thereby extending their lifetime.

The XRD patterns of the obtained porous carbon under different calcination conditions are shown in Fig. S7a. The diffraction peak of 21° is detected in XRD pattern of porous carbon, which corresponds to diffraction plane indices (002) [39]. Moreover, no other diffraction peaks were observed, proving that the obtained porous carbon maintained high purity.

FT-IR was employed to analyze the functional groups on the porous carbon (Fig. S7b and Fig. S7c). In the FT-IR spectrum, –OH (3436 cm−1), –CH3 (2921 cm−1), –CH2- (2850 cm−1), and CC (1654 cm−1) stretching vibration can be detected. Moreover, the peak at 1106 cm−1 was assigned to the C–O asymmetric stretching vibration of group (-C-O-C-). Here, whether employing the enzymolysis-treatment for porous carbon does not influence the functional groups on carriers. Moreover, no new absorption peaks appeared before and after the addition of CQDs in the enzymolysis-treated samples, indicating that there was only physical interaction between the samples and CQDs.

As is well-known, the pore structure of the supporting materials has a non-negligible influence on the energy storage efficiency of composite PCMs [49]. Normally, micro-sized pores can efficiently improve the adsorption of PCMs molecules through physical forces such as capillary force and surface tension, while mesopores and macropores can provide luxuriant transport channels for melted PCMs molecules. In order to explore the effect of enzymolysis-treatment and CQDs loading on the specific surface area and pore structure of wood-derived porous carbon, an isothermal nitrogen adsorption and desorption test was carried out. The resulting 7 groups of samples all showed type IV adsorption curves (Fig. S8). For the delignificated porous carbon (Fig. S8a), except for the sample of CW-D-700, there is a certain amount of adsorption in the low relative pressure area with P/P0 < 0.1. Among them, CW-D-900 has the largest adsorption capacity, and an obvious hysteresis loop appears in the middle relative pressure zone, indicating the existence of the mesoporous structure, while in the high relative pressure zone, the adsorption capacity increased rapidly, which proves that the carbon material contains a highly open pore structure. As is shown in Table S2, for the delignificated porous carbon, the pore volume changes from 0.467 to 0.258, 0.685 cm3g-1, as the pyrolysis temperature increases from 700 °C, 800 °C–900 °C, respectively. Meanwhile, the specific surface area also significantly transforms from 588.0, 240.7–893.3 m2 g−1. Moreover, the pore size distribution curves in Fig. S8b reveal that the pore content is significantly enhanced as the calcination temperature increases. The highest content of micropores is around 0.74 nm, and the mesopores are widely distributed in the range of 2–8 nm. The measurement results reveal that the carbonization temperature decisively influences the pore structure of the delignificated porous carbon. Furthermore, for the enzymolysis-treated porous carbon, the N2 adsorption and desorption curves drastically rise comparing with the delignificated porous carbon in the low relative pressure area of P/P0 < 0.1, indicating that the enzymolysis-treatment step efficiently provides luxuriant micropores for the wood-derived porous carbon (Fig. S8c), which is consistent with their pore size distribution test result (Fig. S8d). Specifically, the pore volume of enzymolysis-treated porous carbon increases from 0.610 to 0.625, 0.644 cm3g-1 as the pyrolysis temperature increases. Simultaneously, the specific surface area transforms from 1289.1, 1002.9–1038.0 m2 g−1 with the increase of pyrolysis temperature (Table S2). Interestingly, the specific surface area of sample CQDs/CW-E−900 is lower than that of CW-E−900, which is attributed to the introduction of CQDs that will occupy sectional pores. BET analysis demonstrates that the introduction of enzymolysis-treatment is profitable to efficiently constructing nano-scale 3D network carbon with luxuriant micropores, which is also beneficial to efficiently adsorbing more organic PCMs.

The vacuum impregnation method was employed to manufacture shape-stable PCMs (ss-PCMs). Compared with the SEM images of original wood-derived porous carbon, the porous carbon encapsulated with PEG molecules has changed from the scabrous surface to the smooth surface (Fig. S9 and Fig. S10), which reveals that PEG molecules are successfully adsorbed into the porous carbon supporting materials through capillary action and boundary tension. Furthermore, the hydrogen bonding between the porous carbon and PEG further strengthens the interaction, so that the melting PCMs is effectively bound in the channel when the phase change occurs, and leakage is prevented. Here, it is observed that whether the porous carbon is enzymolysis-treated or not has no significant influence on the microscopic morphology of the composite PCMs.

XRD patterns were employed to investigate the crystallization behavior of PEG molecules in wood-derived porous carbon. As shown in Fig. S11a and Fig. S11b, two typical diffraction peaks at 19° and 23° are attributed to PEG. In the achieved wood-derived composite PCMs, all the sharp and strong diffraction peaks corresponding to PEG can be obviously observed, which illustrates that the PEG molecules have been successfully impregnated into the 3D porous carbon. Noticeably, wood-derived porous carbon does not transform the crystal structure of PEG, and PEG still maintains good crystallization behavior, indicating that there is excellent chemical compatibility between carbon materials and PEG molecules. And the aforementioned phenomenon is not influenced by the enzymolysis-treatment process or further adding CQDs on carriers.

FT-IR spectra were employed to investigate the interaction between PEG and wood-derived porous carbon materials. As shown in Fig. S11c and Fig. S11d, the peaks at 965 and 2890 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of C–H, corresponding to –CH2 of PEG; 1110 and 3445 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of C–O and –OH of PEG, respectively [18]. All the characteristic peaks attributed to PEG were also detected in the FT-IR spectra of the composite PCMs, including C–H, C–O and –OH. Noticeably, the mentioned phenomenon appeared whether the porous carbon is enzymolysis-treated or further added CQDs on supports. Significantly, no obvious new peaks were found in the FT-IR spectra of the achieved composite, and the peak positions of the composite PCMs and the pure PEG were basically identical, indicating that only physical interaction existed between porous carbons and PEG molecules. This physical interaction succeeded in firmly adsorbing the PEG molecules in porous channels, thereby preventing the leakage of the melting PEG.

DSC measurement was adopted to evaluate the thermal properties of wood-derived composite PCMs, and the detailed thermal performance data are summarized in Table S3. The phase change enthalpy is the most dependable indicator to evaluate the thermal performance of composite PCMs. As shown in Fig. S12 and Table S3, comparing PEG@CW-700 and PEG@CW-D-700, the delignification treatment can increase the melting enthalpy and solidification enthalpy of the composite, which proves the importance of the delignification step to increase the enthalpy value. Moreover, the enthalpy value of the porous carbon without enzymolysis-treatment increases with the increase of the calcination temperature. Nevertheless, the enthalpy value of enzymolysis-treated porous carbon first decreases and then increases with the increase of the calcination temperature, which is coincident with the variation tendency of the pore structure. Unquestionably, the enzymolysis-treatment for materials has unprecedentedly improved the energy storage capacity of the complexes, and the enthalpy value of PEG@CW-E−900 is as high as 151.74 J g−1. Regretfully, the loading of CQDs in the carrier makes the composite PCMs slightly lose some enthalpy value, only reaching 144.51 J g−1, but the acquisition of PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 still provides the possibility for the preparation of fluorescent functionalized composite PCMs. Furthermore, the thermal properties of the achieved composite PCMs and the wood-based composite PCMs reported in previous literatures were compared, and it was concluded that the introduction of enzymolysis-treatment with the advantages of environmental friendliness and simple process unprecedentedly promoted the energy storage efficiency of wood-based composite PCMs (Table S4).

Thermal stability is a very significant indicator to evaluate the practical application value of composite PCMs. Considering that the enzymolysis-treated wood-derived porous carbon displays a more prominent loading performance for pure PEG molecules, compared to the porous carbon without enzymolysis treatment. Therefore, we particularly investigated the thermal stability of the porous carbon-based composite PCMs with enzymolysis treatment. The TGA and DTG thermograms of wood-derived porous carbon, pure PEG2000, and composite PCMs are exhibited in Fig. S13. One weight loss stage of pure PEG and composite PCMs can be observed during the heating process (Fig. S13a), which is ascribed to the pyrolyzation of PEG molecules. The pyrolysis initiatory temperature of pure PEG and composite PCMs is about 200 °C in TG curves, and the terminative temperature is about 450 °C. Unquestionably, the resulting composite PCMs exhibit prominent thermal stability when the program temperature is lower than 200 °C. Corresponding to DTG (Fig. S13b and Fig. S13d), PEG@CW-E−700, PEG@CW-E−800, PEG@CW-E−900, and PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 have the fastest temperature weight loss at 389.4, 387.0, 387.3, and 371.8 °C, respectively, which is lower than that of pure PEG at 392.7 °C, and the account for the slight deviation of the temperature with the fastest weight loss rate to the low temperature is correlative to the introduction of the 3D hierarchical porous carbon supports. Furthermore, the residual amount of PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 after pyrolysis is lower than that of PEG@CW-E−900 (Fig. S13c), which is caused by the CQDs loaded in PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 occupying the sectional pores in carriers. Integrally speaking, the practical encapsulation rate of PEG in the composite PCMs depending on the mass loss during the thermal decomposition process is essentially coincident with the theoretical impregnation rate of PEG. The weight loss caused by wood-derived porous carbon in pyrolysis process below 700 °C is almost inappreciable, suggesting that they have obvious advantages as supporting materials in exploiting composite PCMs with good thermal stability.

Thermal conductivity is a particularly important indicator of composite PCMs. As is well-known, the thermal conduction of non-metallic materials is primarily dominated by the thermal vibration of the crystal lattice, namely, phonons [50]. The highly conjugated π-π bond in the carbon materials is advantageous to boost its thermal vibration, thus enhancing the thermal conductivity of the composite PCMs. As shown in Fig. S14, the thermal conductivities of wood-derived composite PCMs are higher than pure PEG (0.207 Wm−1K−1) to varying degrees. Moreover, as the calcination temperature increases, the thermal conductivity of the composite PCMs gradually enhances. It is worth noting that the improvement of thermal conductivity by the introduction of porous carbon supports in this work is slight, which can be attributed to the high loading ratio of PEG in the obtained composite PCMs, as well as the not high enough thermal conductivity of wood-derived carbon. Interestingly, the addition of CQDs on porous carbon will slightly increase the thermal conductivity, which may be attributed to the characteristic electronic structure on the surface of CQDs that facilitates thermal vibration of the carbon materials.

Leakage testing was also employed to determine the thermal stability of wood-derived composite PCMs and pure PEG manufactured by melting-impregnation method. As shown in Fig. S15, when the heating temperature gradually increased from room temperature to 90 °C, the camera recorded photos of the PEG and the compounds rising to different temperatures. Pure PEG gradually melts when the temperature rises to near the melting point of PEG. However, all prepared composite PCMs maintain excellent shape stability, indicating that porous carbon triumphantly confines the PEG molecules in the channels through capillary action and hydrogen bonding force. Therefore, when the temperature exceeds the melting point of PEG, the liquid PEG can still be effectively trapped in the pores of the porous carbon without leakage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-isothermal crystallization kinetics process of composite PCMs
ce:section.ce:para: Composite PCMs undergo the phase transition under the influence of environmental temperature evolves, that is, from crystalline to amorphous to crystalline, and the process is reversible. Therefore, it is of great significance to investigate the phase transition kinetics of PEG in the channels of porous carbon. Considering that the thermal performance of composite PCMs intensely relies on the phase transition behavior of pure PEG, we deliberatively choose PEG and PEG/CW-E−900 to investigate the non-isothermal phase transition kinetics (Table S5 and Table S6). Generally, the phase change process of PEG is predominantly regulated by the homogeneous nucleation and crystal growth mechanism, while the phase change process of PEG/CW-E−900 is primarily controlled by the heterogeneous nucleation and crystal growth mechanism.

The DSC curves of PEG and PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs at different melting/freezing rates are shown in Fig. 3a–d. With the increase of the cooling rate, the initial crystallization temperature (T
0 ) and the crystallization peak temperature (T
p ) of the PEG and PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs shift to the low temperature direction, and the half crystallization time is shortened [51]. The phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that the rate of movement of the PEG molecular chains is smaller than the rate of temperature change, which results in the hindrance of the crystallization process. Therefore, the degree of supercooling required for PEG crystallization is increased, thereby reducing T
0 and T
p . Moreover, the increased degree of supercooling will enhance the nucleation rate, thus reducing the degree of crystallization perfection and shortening the half-crystallization time. Comparing Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b, the introduction of the supporting materials broadened the half-width (
Δ
W) of the crystallization peak of PEG, indicating that the crystallization performance, crystal uniformity and uniformity of the grain distribution of the achieved composite PCMs are slightly reduced compared to pure PEG [52,53]. Noticeably, the crystallization peaks of pure PEG changed from one to two in Fig. 3a, which may be attributed to the incomplete crystallization of PCMs caused by too fast melting/freezing rate. However, the composite PCMs all maintained a crystallization peak during the crystallization process, which may be attributed to the fact that the support provides abundant nucleation sites for the crystallization of PCMs, promoting the homogeneous nucleation of PCMs molecules in the pores of the support.

The thermal cycling stability of composite PCMs is very important for their industrial applications. The FT-IR spectra, temperature-time curve, DSC curves, and XRD patterns of PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs before and after 100 times thermal cycling are exhibited inFig. 3e–h. It can be deduced that the performance indicators of the composite PCMs before and after 100 thermal cycles have no obvious variation. Distinctly, the structural stability of wood-derived 3D network carbon and the prominent compatibility between the carriers and PEG together confer the excellent thermal cycling stability for composite PCMs.

The transient temperature response of the composite PCMs measured by the infrared thermal imager can be employed to evaluate the corresponding thermal transfer performance. Herein, the transient temperature response of the composite PCMs were recorded by an infrared thermal imager during heating process, which were taken after a fixed time on a hot plate at 80 °C. Infrared thermal imaging photos (Fig. 3i) show that the PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs has a faster heating rate than PGE for the same heating time, which reveals that the wood-derived 3D interconnected network carbon with hierarchical porous structure effectively facilitates the thermal conduction capacity of PCMs.

The relative crystallinity (F
r ) of PEG molecules in wood-based composite PCMs at different temperatures (T) can be calculated by formula (1):
(1)
F r
=
∫
T 0
T
d H
d T
d T
∫
T 0
T ∞
d H
d T
d T
× 100 %
where
T 0
and
T ∞
respectively represent the initial crystallization temperature and the complete crystallization temperature of PEG molecules in composite PCMs, and H represents the melting enthalpy of the composite PCMs. Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b exhibit the relationship between crystallization temperature (T) and F
r at different freezing rates. With the increase of the freezing rate, the span of the temperature range included in the completion of crystallization of pure PEG and composite PCMs is prolonged, which can be ascribe to the augment in the degree of supercooling caused by the excessively fast temperature change restraining the crystallization of PEG molecules. Noteworthily, the temperature range for the completion of crystallization of the PEG/CW-E−900 is shortened compared with pure PEG, which is attributed to the effective increase of thermal transfer efficiency by the introduction of porous carbon-based supporting materials.

For the non-isothermal crystallization process, the crystallization time (t) has the following relationship with temperature by formula (2):
(2)
t=(T
0 -T)/Φ
where T is the temperature at time t, andΦ represents different freezing rates. The T-F
r curve shown in Fig. 4a and b can be converted into a t-F
r curve by formula (2), as shown in Fig. 4d and e. From Fig. 4c, the half crystallization time (t 1/2) of PEG and composite PCMs can be calculated (Table S5 and Table S6). As the freezing rate Φ increases, t 1/2 decreases (Fig. 4c), which is due to the increase in freezing rate that shortens the nucleation and growth time of PEG crystals. Moreover, for the identical freezing rate (Φ = 2 °C/min), the introduction of the carriers significantly shortens the crystallization time of PEG and increases the crystallization rate of PEG, which are mainly due to the carriers acting as the nucleating agents for PEG molecules. However, for the increased freezing rate (Φ = 5 °C/min, 10 °C/min, 15 °C/min, and 20 °C/min), the crystallization time of PEG in the complex is prolonged, which is caused by the fact that the movement rate of PEG in the confined space of the carriers cannot keep up with the rate of temperature change.

Zhishen Mo method as a frequently-used method to investigate thoroughly the non-isothermal crystallization kinetics of polymers was employed to further explore the influence of wood-based porous carriers on the crystallization kinetics of PEG molecules [54]. Mo et al. integrated the Avrami equation and Ozawa equation to deal with non-isothermal crystallization kinetics, and obtained the following equation (3):
(3) InΦ = InF(T)-aInt
where F(T) represents the freezing rate (K/min) that must be selected when the system reaches a certain relative crystallinity per unit crystallization time, that is, the degree of difficulty for the sample to reach a certain crystallinity within a certain crystallization time. Detailedly, the larger the value of F(T), the slower the crystallization rate of the system, and the smaller the value of F(T), the faster the crystallization rate of the system. Furthermore, when the crystallinity of different systems is the same, the time it consumes for diverse freezing rates to reach the crystallinity is various. Therefore, a series of curves with disparate crystallinity can be acquired by plotting InΦ to Int, and the slope and intercept of the fitted line represent a and F(T) respectively (Table S7). The relative crystallinity selected in Fig. 4g and h is 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% respectively, and the relationship curves of InΦ-Int of pure ·PEG and PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs all exhibit a good linear relationship, indicating that the non-isothermal crystallization process of the composites can be better described by Zhishen Mo's method [55]. With the increase of the relative crystallinity F
r , the F(T) value of pure PEG and the composites increases, which means that if the relative crystallinity F
r of the material is to be increased, the freezing rate Φ needs to be accelerated. Namely, the larger the F(T), the slower the crystallization rate, and the larger the t 1/2.

Furthermore, the F(T) value of the complexes decreased first (F
r = 20%) and then increased (F
r = 40%∼100%) compared with pure PEG, indicating that the carriers act as the nucleating agent in the initial stage of crystallization of PEG molecules shortening the nucleation time, and the inherent spatial confinement effect of the supporting materials inhibits the growth of PEG crystals in the later crystallization stage. Summarily, the crystal growth process of PEG molecules in the composites is the consequence of the combined effect of the promotion of initial nucleation and the inhibition of crystallization caused by the spatial confinement effect.

The phase transition kinetics of porous carbon-based composite PCMs is predominantly determined by two factors: first, the carriers can act as the heterogeneous nucleating agent during the phase transition of the PCMs, thereby generating positive effects; second, the supports hinder the free migration and diffusion of PCMs molecular chains, and limit the growth of PCMs crystals, thereby producing negative effects [56]. Generally, the activation energy reflects the phase change energy barrier of PCMs under non-isothermal phase change kinetics. Activation energy is related to the nucleation and migration process of PCMs molecules, so activation energy includes nucleation activation energy and transmission activation energy. The nucleation activation energy is the energy required to form the critical size crystal nucleus, and the transmission activation energy is the energy used to transport PCMs molecules across the phase interface [57]. The activation energy ΔE
a of the non-isothermal phase transition is calculated by Kissinger formula (4):
(4)

Δ

)
where T
p represents the phase transition temperature, A is the constant factor, and R is the gas constant. ΔE
a can be calculated from the slope of the curve of ln(Φ/Tp 2) and 1/T
p . Fig. 4f is the ln(Φ/Tp 2) and 1/T
p linear fitting curves of pure PEG and CW-E−900 composite PCMs obtained by the Kissinger kinetic method [58]. The regression curve of pure PEG is y = 9.59–884.93x, the slope is –E/R = −884.93 kJ mol−1, and the activation energy of pure PEG is calculated to be E = 7.36 kJ mol−1. The curve of the composites is y = 1.79–487.77x, the slope is –E/R = −487.77, and the activation energy of the complex is calculated to be E = 4.06 kJ mol−1. Noteworthily, the activation energy of the composite PCMs is lower than that of pure PEG (Fig. 4i), which indicates that the porous carbon-encapsulated PEG molecules are more aptitudinal to phase transition, and heterogeneous nucleation facilitates a lower activation energy barrier. As mentioned above, wood-derived porous carbon can effectively promote the heterogeneous nucleation of PEG molecules and reduce the activation energy of nucleation, while the porous carbon also can restrain the free movement of PEG chains and increase the transmission activation energy. Apparently, the heterogeneous nucleation effect of PEG molecules exceeds the hindering effect of its molecular movement after wood-derived porous carbon encapsulates PEG molecules, that is, the aforementioned negative effects have been effectively suppressed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-to-thermal and electric-to-thermal conversion capabilities of composite PCMs
ce:section.ce:para:
As is well-known, carbon-based materials have exceedingly strong absorption of light and exhibit good light-to-thermal conversion capabilities. In order to evaluate the light-to-thermal conversion capability of composite PCMs, the achieved samples were illuminated by a 300 W xenon lamp that simulates sunlight, and the infrared temperature probe of a paperless recorder was employed to monitor and record the temperature evolution of the samples during the illumination process (Fig. 5 a). Wood-derived porous carbon carriers exhibit outstanding solar absorption and transmission properties in light-to-thermal conversion applications. Under the premise of ensuring prominent mechanical properties, the acquired carriers can transmit visible light radiation, so that invisible light in the infrared part of the spectrum is absorbed and converted into thermal energy. At the moment, the supporting materials provide 3D thermal transmission paths for the organic PCMs, which contribute to the enhanced thermal storage efficiency of the composite. As shown in Fig. 5b, with the increase of light intensity, the maximum temperature of PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs reached in about 10 min gradually increased. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that light with a higher intensity accelerates the thermal movement of the phonon lattice, causing the composite to rise to a higher temperature per unit of time (Fig. 5b). Besides, the effect of the addition of CQDs on the photo-thermal conversion performance of PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs was explored. The results (Fig. S16a) showed that there was almost no obvious difference between PEG@CW-E−900 and PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900. The photo-thermal energy conversion efficiency can be calculated by the equation (5) as following [59]:
(5)
η =
m Δ H
ρ S
(
t t
−
t f
)

Here, m is the mass of the sample,
Δ H
is the latent heat obtained by DSC,
ρ
is the intensity of light irradiation of the simulated solar light source, S is the surface area of sample,
t t
and
t f
are the starting and terminating time before and after the phase transition, respectively. The calculated photo-thermal energy conversion efficiency of PEG@CW-E−900 and PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 is 81.2% and 80.3%, respectively. The difference is mainly attributed to the fact that the addition of a small amount of CQDs slightly reduced the absorption of visible light, which can be demonstrated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. S17).

The combination of PCMs and the conductive supporting materials can realize electric driving while maintaining the shape stability of PCMs, which provides the possibility for the application of composite PCMs in electronic devices and other fields. The electro-thermal conversion mechanism of the composite PCMs is that the moving electrons collide with other molecules or groups when the current passes through the conductive composites, thereby generating Joule heat. The released Joule heat, that is, the heat generated by the resistance, is stored in the form of latent heat by PCMs (Fig. 5e). The schematic diagram of the electro-thermal test system is shown in Fig. 5c, the temperature probe of the paperless recorder can record the instantaneous temperature response of PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs under different voltages in real time. As shown in Fig. 5d, the temperature of PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs reached 43.2 °C after 28 min excitation with 3 V voltage, and 54.0 °C after 28 min excitation with 5 V voltage. Distinctly, the 3 V voltage excitation is not enough to reach the phase transition temperature of the composite PCMs, and no phase transition occurs. However, the partial phase transition occurs in the composite PCMs when a voltage of 5 V is excited, so the achieved composites only reserve part of the latent heat. Moreover, the effect of the addition of CQDs on the electro-thermal energy conversion performance of PEG/CW-E−900 composite PCMs was also explored. The results (Fig. S16b) showed that the electro-thermal conversion curves of PEG@CW-E−900 and PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 are almost indistinguishable, but the maximum temperature that PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 can reach is slightly higher than that of PEG@CW-E−900. The phenomenon is mainly attributed to the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in the CQDs, which facilitates the transport of electrons in the PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 composite and then affects the thermal vibrations of phonons. The electro-thermal conversion efficiency (η) could be calculated by equation (6), [60]:
(6)
η =
m Δ H
U I

Δ H
and m are the latent heat obtained by DSC and mass of the sample, respectively. U is the voltage imposed to samples, and I is the current.

t f
are the starting and terminating time before and after the phase transition, respectively. The calculated electro-thermal conversion efficiency of PEG@CW-E−900 and PEG@CQDs/CW-E−900 is 66.7% and 68.4%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of composite PCMs in battery temperature regulation system
ce:section.ce:para: With the increasingly serious problems of resource shortage and environmental pollution, the development of new energy vehicles has become the predominant trend and basic policy of automobile production and use in various countries in the world. As everyone knows, the temperature of the power battery fluctuates greatly during the operation of the car, which poses safety risks and also affects the service life of the battery [61,62]. In order to effectively solve the problem of battery temperature instability and extend battery life, researchers attempt to apply PCMs to the temperature regulation technology of lithium batteries, and to improve the thermal transfer performance of the battery by adding additives such as metal, graphite, and cellulose [63]. However, most of the PCMs currently employed in battery temperature regulation (BTR) have drawbacks such as poor mechanical properties, high cost, complicated manufacture process, heavy weight, and unsatisfactory thermal storage efficiency, which restrict the further development of PCMs in the field of BTR. Herein, we apply the resulting wood-derived composite PCMs to the BTR system. Particularly, the outstanding mechanical properties and anisotropic thermal transfer capabilities of the composite PCMs are employed to give the BTR system the maximum thermal transfer rate in anticipant direction. The proposed possible BTR system is shown in Fig. 6 . It is well known that the higher the loading rate of pure PCMs in the composite PCMs, the higher the energy storage density, but this will competitively affect the thermal conductivity of the composites. Therefore, the key to design battery thermal management composites is to reasonably select the encapsulation rate of composite PCMs to take into account both thermal conductivity and energy storage density. This research provides an effective idea for designing a new BER system, with a view to further efficiently shorten the temperature fluctuation range of the battery system and prolong the service life of the battery in the future.

A template-free, top-down method was employed to manufacture 3D wood-derived hierarchical porous carbon. Cellulase was used to partially hydrolyze the cellulose in wood and construct extremely abundant adsorption sites after high-temperature pyrolysis. The synergistic effect of enzymatic hydrolysis and high-temperature pyrolysis realizes the regulation of the microscopic pore structure of the carriers, so as to better encapsulate the PEG and fully release the crystallization behavior. This research constructs a multifunctional shape-stable PCM system with visible light capture, photo-thermal conversion, electro-thermal conversion, thermal energy storage and fluorescence functions, which implements the high-efficiency conversion of visible light and thermal energy storage, broadens the application field of energy storage, improves the efficiency of photo-to-thermal conversion.
